diagrams.title: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) - Kruskal's Algorithm
diagrams.description: Shows a weighted undirected graph with 6 vertices and demonstrates Kruskal's algorithm step-by-step by selecting the minimum weight edges that don't form cycles. The diagram displays the original graph with edge weights, then shows the MST being built edge by edge until all vertices are connected with minimum total weight.
elements: MST edges highlighted in different color
diagrams.purpose: Explains how Kruskal's algorithm uses greedy approach to find MST by always selecting the minimum weight edge that doesn't create a cycle

diagrams.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm
diagrams.description: Illustrates a weighted directed graph with 5 vertices and demonstrates Dijkstra's algorithm finding shortest paths from a source vertex (A). Shows the priority queue/table updating distances, visited vertices marked, and the final shortest path tree with distances from source to each vertex.
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates how Dijkstra's algorithm uses greedy approach to find shortest paths from a single source to all other vertices in a graph with non-negative weights

diagrams.title: Greedy Choice Property Comparison
diagrams.description: Shows a simple graph demonstrating the greedy choice property - making locally optimal choice (selecting minimum weight edge) leading to global optimum. Compares greedy solution vs non-greedy solution to illustrate why greedy works for MST and shortest path problems.
diagrams.purpose: Visualizes the core concept of greedy algorithms - making locally optimal decisions to achieve global optimum, fundamental to understanding why MST and shortest path algorithms work
